8 min read
Just now

--

The era of clicking, tapping, and visually verifying our digital actions is over. But when the OS does everything for you in the background, who is really in control?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

At WWDC 2026, Apple quietly ended the era of static applications. Welcome to the post-app interface, where software has no buttons.
Picture the scene: The lights dim in the Steve Jobs Theater. A sleek, animated graphic morphs on the massive screen behind the executives. The announcement is made. From now on, you don’t need to navigate through grids of apps to get things done. You don’t need to open your banking app, switch to your calendar, and then hop into your email.
You just ask, and the system does.
The crowd in Cupertino erupted into cheers. Tech journalists furiously typed out headlines about the frictionless future and the ultimate AI assistant.
But in private Slack channels, Discord servers, and corporate security ops centers across the globe, a very different reaction was taking place. Cybersecurity researchers went dead silent. Then, the panic set in.
For over a decade, our digital security relied on one fundamental, undeniable human action: The Click. Before money was transferred, before an email was sent, or before a file was…
